findings.title: Empty-chunk `continue` skips resetting `last_chunk_ends_with_cr`
findings.detail:
When `chunk` becomes empty after stripping the leading `
` (line 813), the code `continue`s at line 816 — jumping back to the `for` loop header **without** executing line 819 (`last_chunk_ends_with_cr = chunk.endswith(carriage_return)`). This means `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` remains `True`. If the *next* chunk also starts with `
`, that `
` would be incorrectly swallowed as well, producing wrong output.

Here's a concrete example that demonstrates the bug:
  chunks: `[b'a', b'
', b'
b']`
  Expected (matches `b'a

b'.splitlines()`): `[b'a', b'', b'b']`
  Actual with this code: `[b'a', b'b']` — the second `
` (start of the third chunk) is incorrectly eaten.

Fix: reset `last_chunk_ends_with_cr = False` before the `continue` on line 816.

findings.title: `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` not reset when delimiter branch is taken
findings.detail: The `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` flag is only updated inside the `else` branch (no delimiter). When a `delimiter` is specified (line 800), the flag is neither read nor cleared, so its stale value lingers from any prior iteration that might have gone through the `else` branch (e.g. if a caller switched branches, or the flag was set on a prior chunk when there was no delimiter and then delimiter was set — unlikely in normal usage, but still a latent bug). More importantly, if `iter_lines` is called without a delimiter for a few chunks and then some edge case triggers the delimiter path, the stale flag could cause incorrect behavior. Consider setting `last_chunk_ends_with_cr = False` inside the `if delimiter:` branch to keep the invariant clean.

findings.title: `carriage_return` / `line_feed` are computed but `carriage_return` is unused when `decode_unicode` is falsy in a delimiter path
findings.detail: `carriage_return` is only used on line 819 (inside the `else`/no-delimiter branch). `line_feed` is only used on line 809 (same branch). These variables are set unconditionally but are only meaningful in the `else` branch. This is harmless but slightly wasteful. A minor style nit.

findings.title: Test case `[b'a\r', b'\nb\r']` expected no-delimiter result changed silently
findings.detail: The old expected result for `[b'a\r', b'\nb\r']` was `['a', '', 'b']` (three items), and the new expected result is `[u'a', u'b']` (two items). While this matches the new correct behavior (the `\r\n` spanning two chunks is now treated as a single line break), the test comment does not call out this behavior change, making it hard for a future reader to understand why the middle empty string was removed. Consider adding a comment explaining the semantic change.

findings.title: `mock_iter_content` still returns bytes from mutated `mock_chunks`, but decode_unicode=True path decodes them
findings.detail: After `mock_chunks = [chunk.replace(b'\n', b'\r') for chunk in mock_chunks]` at line 1351, the `mock_iter_content` closure still references the outer `mock_chunks` variable by name. However, `mock_chunks` is reassigned here (it's a local variable, not a closure over a list mutation). The `mock_iter_content` function defined at line 1322 captures `mock_chunks` from the enclosing scope using a *name lookup*, but since `mock_chunks` is rebound via `=` at line 1351, the closure will correctly see the new list. This is fine in Python — closures look up the name at call time. No bug, just worth a confirming comment.

` would be incorrectly swallowed as well, producing wrong output.

b']`

findings.title: `mock_iter_content` still returns bytes from mutated `mock_chunks`, but decode_unicode=True path decodes them
findings.detail: After `mock_chunks = [chunk.replace(b'\n', b'\r') for chunk in mock_chunks]` at line 1351, the `mock_iter_content` closure still references the outer `mock_chunks` variable by name. However, `mock_chunks` is reassigned here (it's a local variable, not a closure over a list mutation). The `mock_iter_content` function defined at line 1322 captures `mock_chunks` from the enclosing scope using a *name lookup*, but since `mock_chunks` is rebound via `=` at line 1351, the closure will correctly see the new list. This is fine in Python — closures look up the name at call time. No bug, just worth a confirming comment.
